content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 25, 2017

Jolie Houston
Berliner Cohen LLP
Ten Almaden Blvd., 11th Floor
San Jose, CA 95113-2233

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-258

Dear Ms. Houston:

This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of Los Altos Councilmember
Jeannie Bruins, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Bruins pay a percentage share of an application fee for a Single Story
Overlay District encompassing her residence and participate in the mail-in vote process for creating
the district?

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Brums from paying her share of the application
fee or participating in the mail-in vote because neither of these actions would constitute making,
participating in making, or using her position to influence a governmental decision.

FACTS

Councilmember Bruins owns her home in Los Altos. Her home is located in a RI-10 district,
which currently allows for two-story homes. However, all homes within the pertinent neighborhood
including the council members are single story homes, and an application for a Single-Story
Overlay District ("SSOD") was submitted to the City on December 6, 2016.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-258
Page No. 2

A SSOD is a zoning designation that can be adopted to preserve and maintain single-family neighborhoods that are predominantly single story in character. The effect of the SSOD is that the properties within the district are restricted from converting their single-story homes to two-story homes. The City has adopted a procedure for establishing an SSOD, which in short requires:

• A petition from at least 50% of the properties within the proposed SSOD.
• A $4,500 application fee.
• A public mail-in vote requiring 66-2/3% affirmative votes.
• City Council approval.

The SSOD petition for the Councilmember's neighborhood, which includes a total of 47 properties and the Councilmember's residence, has obtained the requisite number of signatures and will now proceed through the SSOD process. Councilmember Bruins has not previously participated in decisions relating to the SSOD or the public effort to create the SSOD. Her name/signature is not on the submitted petition, she is not a member of the organizing group leading the effort, and she has not yet contributed to the application fee.

Councilmember Bruins does not intend to participate as a councilmember when the SSOD application comes before the City Council for consideration and decision. However, Councilmember Bruins would like to make a payment of her percentage share of the SSOD application fee and participate in the mail-in vote process.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Pursuant to Section 87103(b), it appears that Councilmember Bruins has a financial interest in her residence as a real property interest of $2,000 or more. Therefore, Councilmember Bruins is prohibited from "making," "participating in making" or using her position to "influence" a governmental decision with a foreseeable and material effect on her residence.

For purposes of the conflict of interest provisions, a public official "makes" a governmental decision when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, authorizes or directs any action, votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704(a).) A public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or she provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation in order to affect the decision without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b).) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to "influence" a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(1).)"

¹ For a decision not before the official's own agency, an official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision if the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency and acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency for the purpose of influencing the decision. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).)

File No. A-16-258
Page No. 3

However, a public official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a decision
if the official makes an appearance as a member of the general public before an agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function on matters solely related to the official's personal
interests. (Regulation 18704(d)(2).) Moreover, we have previously advised that in limited
circumstances an official may avail himself of the same procedures available to the public. (See
Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-229.)

You have stated that Councilmember Bruins does not intend to take part in the SSOD
process in her official capacity. While we caution that she should not contact other city officers or
employees in regards to the SSOD application, Councilmember Bruins is not making, participating
in making or using her official position to influence a decision if she pays her share of the
application fee or takes part in the public vote.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

sections.question:
May Councilmember Bruins pay a percentage share of an application fee for a Single Story 
Overlay District encompassing her residence and participate in the mail-in vote process for creating 
the district?

fee or participating in the mail-in vote because neither of these actions would constitute making, 
participating in making, or using her position to influence a governmental decision.

QUESTION: May Councilmember Bruins pay a percentage share of an application fee for a Single Story 
Overlay District encompassing her residence and participate in the mail-in vote process for creating 
the district?

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit Councilmember Bums from paying her share of the application 
fee or participating in the mail-in vote because neither of these actions would constitute making, 
participating in making, or using her position to influence a governmental decision.
